hero-title: How you remember, and how a machine does
hero-lede: The recall cards on this site keep a little bit of information — what you remembered, what you let slip, and when each idea is due to come round again. None of it ever leaves your browser. Here it all is, drawn as a picture: your own forgetting curve, and the ladder your cards slowly climb. Then flip the switch and watch how very differently a machine "remembers" the same thing.

These cards keep a little data — what stuck, what slipped, when each idea is due — none of it leaving your browser. Drawn as a picture: your forgetting curve and the ladder your cards climb. Flip the switch for how differently a machine "remembers" the same thing.
explain-you: Each time you bring a card back to mind, it climbs one rung — and the wait before you see it next grows longer: a day, then three, then a week, then months. Forget one, and it slides right back down to the bottom. That widening ladder is your memory growing stronger; the drops are forgetting, made visible.

Each time you recall a card, it climbs a rung — and the gap before its next visit widens: a day, three, a week, then months. Forget one and it drops back to the bottom. That widening ladder is your memory getting stronger; the drops are forgetting, made visible.

Recall a card, it climbs a rung; the gap widens — a day, three, a week, months. Forget it, it drops to the bottom. The widening ladder is memory strengthening; the drops are forgetting, made visible.
explain-llm: A model has no ladder at all. The very same items sit together in a flat store, looked up the instant they are needed and left alone the rest of the time. Asking for them doesn't make them stronger; time passing doesn't make them fade. It isn't really remembering — it's retrieval.

A model has no ladder. The same items sit in a flat store, looked up the instant they're needed and otherwise left alone. Asking doesn't strengthen them; time doesn't fade them. It isn't remembering — it's retrieval.

A model has no ladder. The same items sit in a flat store, looked up the instant they're needed and otherwise untouched. Asking doesn't strengthen them; time doesn't fade them. It isn't remembering — it's retrieval.

A model has no ladder. The same items sit in a flat store, looked up the instant they're needed, otherwise untouched. Asking doesn't strengthen them; time doesn't fade them. Not remembering — retrieval.
A model has no ladder. Items sit in a flat store, looked up on demand, otherwise untouched. Asking doesn't strengthen them; time doesn't fade them. Not remembering — retrieval.
make-note: No data yet, or would you like to watch it move? Answer a card — honestly — and the picture above will update right on the spot. Two of them are about memory itself, so the simple act of answering is also the lesson.

bridge-working: Hold a phone number in your head while you dial it: that's working memory — just a handful of things at once, gone in the blink of an eye. A model has its own version of this, the context window: everything in the conversation right now, held all at once, and just as gone the moment the chat ends. Both are small and both slip away — which is why we lean on outside scaffolding to hold what won't fit.

bridge-consolidate: Past that first blink, the two paths split apart. You move things into long-term memory by consolidating — each time you retrieve an idea you rebuild it and lay the trace down a little deeper — though each rebuild can also quietly reshape it. A model doesn't consolidate anything from talking with you; between sessions it keeps nothing at all. To carry knowledge forward it retrieves from an external store that someone built. The lookup is fast and tireless — but the judgement of what was worth keeping stayed human.

next-note: Two short notes go a little deeper on the human half: retrieval beats re-reading and spacing fights forgetting. Come back to this page whenever you like — the more cards you answer across the site, the more it becomes a picture of your memory, rather than just an example.
